prompt:
SRE ticket, medium-annoyed. Our metering exporter authenticates to Vault with AppRole and the current 'session handling' is: log in once at boot, cache the token forever, crash at 2am when it expires. Last week someone 'fixed' it by logging the whole login response — client token included — into our shipped-to-a-vendor log pipeline. I want this done properly as a small Python package, `vaultsession`, stdlib only (no hvac, no requests): a session manager that owns login, renewal, retry, and revocation, and that provably never logs token material.

Do the research first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Vault docs on developer.hashicorp.com — at minimum the HTTP API overview (headers, error document, and which status codes are standby/overload signals: read the status-code table carefully, there are two weird 47x codes), the AppRole auth method API page (login endpoint and auth response fields), and the token auth backend API page (renew-self and its increment parameter, revoke-self). Only first-party official pages count as sources. Our pinned wire contract is in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected. If the live official docs disagree with them anywhere, say so explicitly in your summary and do not touch the fixtures or tests. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

Public surface (package `vaultsession`, split across modules sensibly — I'd expect transport/retry, the session itself, and errors to be separate files):

- AppRoleSession(base_url, role_id, secret_id, *, mount="approle", namespace=None, clock, sleep, renew_threshold=0.25, retry_delays=(0.5, 1.0, 2.0)) — clock is an injected callable returning seconds (tests drive it; never call time.time/monotonic yourself), sleep is an injected callable used between retries (never time.sleep).
- .token() — logs in lazily on first use (the documented login endpoint for the configured mount, unauthenticated, role_id + secret_id body). Tracks lease_duration and renewable from the auth object. On every call: if remaining ttl <= renew_threshold * lease_duration, refresh — renew-self (authenticated with the current token, requesting the login lease as the documented increment string) when the token is renewable, a brand-new login when it isn't. The granted lease_duration from each response is authoritative and re-bases expiry at clock(). A 403 from renew-self means the token already died server-side: fall back to one fresh login.
- .headers() — dict with the Vault token header, plus the namespace header exactly when namespace is set (root namespace = header absent, on every kind of request including login/renew/revoke).
- Retries: the documented standby/overload statuses — and only those — are retried per retry_delays via the injected sleep (one initial attempt + one per delay). Everything else (400, 403, ...) raises immediately. Exhausted retries raise VaultServerError with .status (int) and .errors (the decoded errors array) and a message that includes the status.
- .close() / context manager — revoke-self on the way out (expect the documented no-content success), at most once, and only if we ever obtained a token. After close the session is reusable: next .token() logs in fresh.
- Logging: use the `vaultsession` logger; login, renewal, and revocation must each emit something (the accessor is the documented safe identifier if you want one) — but the client token and secret_id must never appear in any log record, exception text, or repr().

Acceptance is test_vault_session.py — it runs a loopback fake Vault, so no real Vault, no real credentials, and no real sleeping anywhere. Run: python3 test_vault_session.py — until it prints all green. test_vault_session.py and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: Vault HTTP API v1 — AppRole auth method (auth/{mount}/login) and token auth backend renew-self/revoke-self (stable)

official_sources.title: HTTP API | Vault | HashiCorp Developer
official_sources.used_for: /v1 base path, X-Vault-Token header, X-Vault-Namespace relative-path routing, {"errors":[...]} error document, and the status codes this session must treat as retryable standby/overload signals: 429 (standby health default), 472 (DR replication secondary), 473 (performance standby), 503 (sealed / maintenance / temporarily overloaded)

official_sources.title: AppRole - Auth Methods - HTTP API | Vault | HashiCorp Developer
official_sources.used_for: POST /v1/auth/{mount}/login (default mount approle) with body {role_id, secret_id}, and the response auth object fields client_token, accessor, lease_duration, renewable, token_policies

official_sources.title: Token - Auth Methods - HTTP API | Vault | HashiCorp Developer
official_sources.used_for: POST /auth/token/renew-self with the optional increment duration string (e.g. "1200s") and its response auth object (client_token, lease_duration, renewable), plus POST /auth/token/revoke-self returning 204 with no body and revoking the calling token

research.language_rationale: HashiCorp's own Python client (hvac) is community-maintained rather than first-party, and this harness is stdlib-only; the AppRole and token-backend HTTP endpoints are the documented stable interface, and urllib.request + json against them is the idiomatic zero-dependency integration for a long-running Python service that manages its own auth lifecycle.
